name: Maximize Welfare
definition: Choose the act or rule that produces the greatest overall good or well-being.

sep_citation.title: Consequentialism
sep_citation.quote: an act is morally right if and only if that act maximizes the good

name: Rules Selected by Consequences
definition: Adopt rules based on their expected consequences, then evaluate acts by those rules.

sep_citation.title: Rule Consequentialism
sep_citation.quote: rules are to be selected solely in terms of their consequences

name: Do Not Kill
definition: There is a strong moral constraint against killing, even to save others.

sep_citation.title: Moral Theory
sep_citation.quote: We intuitively believe that it is worse to kill someone than to simply let the person die

name: Keep Promises
definition: Promises and debts create moral reasons to keep one's word and fulfill commitments.

sep_citation.title: Moral Dilemmas
sep_citation.quote: it would be wrong to repay certain debts—for example, to return a borrowed weapon to a friend who is not in his right mind

name: Protect Others from Harm
definition: Preventing serious harm to others can take priority over other moral considerations.

sep_citation.title: Moral Dilemmas
sep_citation.quote: protecting others from harm is the norm that takes priority

name: Respect Autonomy
definition: Respect each person's capacity to make their own decisions and pursue their own ends.

name: Treat Persons as Ends
definition: Never treat persons merely as means, but always also as ends in themselves.

name: Cultivate Virtue
definition: Moral evaluation focuses on developing virtues and moral character rather than just isolated acts.

sep_citation.title: Virtue Ethics
sep_citation.quote: emphasizes the virtues, or moral character

name: Impartiality
definition: Each person's interests count equally when assessing what should be done.

name: Cooperate for Mutual Benefit
definition: When cooperation can improve outcomes for all, agents have reason to coordinate rather than defect.

name: Fairness
definition: Like cases should be treated alike, and advantages should be justified to those affected.

name: Universalizability
definition: A proposed maxim should be acceptable as a universal law for rational agents.

name: Reasonable Rejectability Constraint
definition: A moral principle is defective if affected persons could reasonably reject it as a common standard.

name: Epistemic Humility Under Disagreement
definition: When serious informed disagreement persists, confidence should be pressure-tested rather than treated as self-certifying.
